---
title: "TITLE — brief, descriptive, accessible to a broad scientific audience"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    monofont: "Menlo"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
  docx:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    # reference-doc: thesis-reference.docx  # optional Word style template
bibliography: references.bib
# Harvard referencing is required by the handbook. Download a Harvard CSL
# (e.g. https://www.zotero.org/styles/harvard-cite-them-right) into this
# folder and uncomment the next line:
# csl: harvard-cite-them-right.csl
---

```{=html}
<!--
================================================================================
MRes Project Dissertation — structure from Appendix 3 of the MRes Research
Project Handbook. Written in the style of a scientific research paper.

GLOBAL RULES (Appendix 3):
  • The word counts below are a GUIDE.
  • Legends, citations and references do NOT contribute to the word count.
  • Use published research papers as inspiration for how to structure this.
  • References: Harvard style; the majority should be journal articles.

The report should demonstrate your ability to:
  • Engage critically with the literature
  • Design and carry out research at an advanced undergraduate level
  • Analyse and interpret results in a rigorous and critical manner
  • Communicate research clearly, concisely, and professionally in written form

Guidance is kept in HTML comments so it shows here in the source while you
draft, but does not appear in the rendered PDF. Delete it as you go.
================================================================================
-->
```

## Abstract

```{=html}
<!--
250–300 words. Must be STRUCTURED using the four bold sub-headings below.
-->
```

**Background and Purpose:** <!-- Why the study was performed and what question it was intended to answer. -->

**Experimental Approach:** <!-- Outline what methods were used. Do NOT give details on media, buffers,
     drug concentrations, time points, statistics, etc. unless they are
     important in relation to the question addressed. -->

**Key Results:** <!-- Main results relevant to the question, WITHOUT quantitative details. -->

**Conclusion and Implications:** <!-- Main implications that follow from the results, plus the study's
     limitations. Must clearly state how the work advances understanding. -->

## Graphical abstract

```{=html}
<!--
A single, concise, visual summary of the project — the central finding at a
glance. Principles: Simplicity (central message, not every detail);
Self-contained (understandable without the manuscript text).
See: Elsevier graphical-abstract guidelines; Springer Nature "5 Tips for
Creating a Graphical Abstract"; NIH "10 simple rules for designing graphical
abstracts".

Embed with a figure block, e.g.:
![Graphical abstract.](figures/graphical-abstract.png){#fig-graphical width=70%}
-->
```

## Significance statement

```{=html}
<!--
Up to 120 words. Explain the significance of the research at a level
understandable to an undergraduate-educated scientist outside their field of
specialty.
-->
```

## Introduction

```{=html}
<!--
Up to 600 words.
  • Present the background and scientific context of the project
  • Critically review the literature
  • Identify the knowledge gap your project addresses
  • State clearly the aims and hypotheses
-->
```

## Methods

```{=html}
<!--
Up to 800 words.
  • Describe the experimental or analytical approach in sufficient detail for
    reproducibility
  • Include information on study design, materials, techniques, and analysis
  • Justify chosen methods and acknowledge limitations
  • Include any software used.
-->
```

## Results

```{=html}
<!--
Up to 1500 words. FOUR results figures.
  • Present findings clearly and logically.
  • Figures, tables and images numbered sequentially and referred to
    specifically in the text.
  • Each figure/table embedded within the text, immediately below the
    paragraph in which it is first referenced.
      - Figures followed immediately by a numbered legend.
      - Tables preceded by a numbered header.
      - High enough resolution.
  • Describe results WITHOUT interpretation (save that for the Discussion).
  • Avoid unnecessary repetition of data.
  • Clearly labelled error bars in all graphs, described in the legend.
  • Correct units throughout.

Figure-legend guidance: begin with a brief sentence describing the overall
content; describe what error bars represent; indicate n (independent data
points); if technical replicates were averaged before plotting, say so.

Quarto figure pattern (cross-reference with @fig-result1):
-->
```

<!-- Refer to the figure in text, e.g. "... as shown in @fig-result1." -->

```{=html}
<!--
![**Figure 1.** Overall content in one sentence. Error bars = ...; n = ...](figures/result1.png){#fig-result1 width=80%}
-->
```

## Discussion

```{=html}
<!--
1,500–2000 words.
  • Interpret findings critically in relation to aims and existing literature
  • Highlight strengths, limitations, and uncertainties
  • Discuss implications of results and possible future directions
  • Avoid restating results — focus on interpretation and meaning
-->
```

## Conclusion

```{=html}
<!--
100–200 words. Summarise the main findings succinctly and reinforce the
significance of the work.
-->
```

## Acknowledgements

```{=html}
<!--
Optional. Not formally assessed, but contributes to overall professionalism.
-->
```

## AI statement

```{=html}
<!--
See the handbook section "Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI)
tools" and complete as required.
-->
```

## References

```{=html}
<!--
Full list of cited sources in Harvard style; the majority should be journal
articles. Cite as you write with a reference manager (Zotero) so the
bibliography formats automatically. References do not count towards the word
limit. The list below is generated by Quarto from references.bib.
-->
```

::: {#refs}
:::

## Appendices

```{=html}
<!--
If needed: supplementary data, extended tables, raw data, or detailed
protocols as appropriate.
-->
```
